items.text: Paleoceno. Paleocene. epoch. Primeiro ciclo turbidítico significativo em Campos; Fm. Carapebus basal

items.title: Eoceno
items.text: Eoceno. Eocene. epoch. Turbiditos eocênicos em Campos (Fm. Carapebus): campo de Marlim, Albacora, Barracuda; início da subsidência acelerada

items.title: Oligoceno
items.text: Oligoceno. Oligocene. epoch. Turbiditos oligocênicos em Campos: Roncador (reservatório principal ~Campaniano a Oligoceno); Fm. Ubatuba (folhelhos pelágicos selantes)

items.title: Neógeno
items.text: Neógeno. Neogene. period. Sedimentação continental e marinha rasa; Fm. Barreiras (Mioceno-Plioceno) na costa nordeste; turbiditos neogênicos em Santos

items.title: Mioceno
items.text: Mioceno. Miocene. epoch. Fm. Barreiras (sedimentação continental costeira); turbiditos miocênicos em bacias da margem equatorial; Bacia do Amazonas offshore

items.title: Plioceno
items.text: Plioceno. Pliocene. epoch. Sedimentação continental/marinha rasa costeira; Fm. Barreiras superior

items.title: Quaternário
items.text: Quaternário. Quaternary. period. Depósitos de cobertura; planícies aluviais, manguezais costeiros; sedimentação atual na plataforma continental

items.title: Pleistoceno
items.text: Pleistoceno. Pleistocene. epoch. Oscilações do nível do mar; exposição da plataforma continental; turbiditos de queda de nível em Campos e Santos

items.title: Holoceno
items.text: Holoceno. Holocene. epoch. Sedimentação atual; mangues, restingas, lamas de plataforma; nível do mar atual

items.title: falha
items.text: falha. fault. A discrete surface or zone of discrete surfaces, with some thickness, separating two rock masses across which one mass has slid past the other, and characterized by brittle deformation.

items.title: falha normal
items.text: falha normal. normal fault. A fault with a dip-slip movement where the hanging wall has moved downward relative to the footwall. Normal faults are associated with extensional tectonic settings.

items.title: falha reversa
items.text: falha reversa. reverse fault. A fault with reverse-slip movement where the hanging wall moves upward relative to the footwall. Dip of fault plane is greater than 45 degrees. Associated with compressional tectonic regimes.

items.title: falha de empurrão
items.text: falha de empurrão. thrust fault. A reverse fault with a shallow dip (typically less than 45 degrees) along which older rocks are thrust over younger rocks. Characteristic of fold-and-thrust belts.

items.title: falha transcorrente
items.text: falha transcorrente. strike-slip fault. A fault where displacement is predominantly parallel to the strike of the fault surface (horizontal movement). Divided into dextral and sinistral varieties based on sense of movement.

items.title: falha transcorrente dextral
items.text: falha transcorrente dextral. dextral strike-slip fault. A strike-slip fault where the block on the opposite side of the fault moves to the right when viewed across the fault plane.

items.title: falha transcorrente sinistral
items.text: falha transcorrente sinistral. sinistral strike-slip fault. A strike-slip fault where the block on the opposite side of the fault moves to the left when viewed across the fault plane.

items.title: falha oblíqua
items.text: falha oblíqua. oblique-slip fault. A fault that has significant components of both dip-slip and strike-slip displacement. Net slip vector is oblique to both strike and dip of the fault surface.

items.title: falha lístrica
items.text: falha lístrica. listric fault. A normal fault that is curved in cross-section, becoming less steep with depth. The fault plane is concave upward, typically flattening into a detachment horizon. Common in rifted continental margins.

items.title: falha de descolamento
items.text: falha de descolamento. detachment fault. A large-displacement, low-angle normal fault that forms the base of an extended terrain. The detachment surface separates an upper plate from a lower plate and may expose metamorphic core complexes.

items.title: falha transformante
items.text: falha transformante. transform fault. A strike-slip fault that is a plate boundary, connecting two other plate boundaries (typically mid-ocean ridges or subduction zones). Movement is parallel to the plate boundary.

items.title: falha transcorrente (crustal)
items.text: falha transcorrente (crustal). transcurrent fault. A large-scale, steeply dipping strike-slip fault that cuts through the crust and may be hundreds of kilometers long. A continental-scale strike-slip fault that crosses structural trends.

items.title: falha cega
items.text: falha cega. blind fault. A fault that does not reach the Earth's surface. The fault terminates below the surface and its displacement is accommodated by folding of the overlying strata, forming a detachment fold.

items.title: falha de crescimento
items.text: falha de crescimento. growth fault. A syn-sedimentary fault that was active during deposition, resulting in thicker strata on the downthrown (hanging wall) side. Common in deltaic and passive margin settings; forms rollover anticlines.

items.title: falha de transferência
items.text: falha de transferência. transfer fault. A fault that transfers displacement between two sub-parallel extensional or contractional fault systems. In rift settings, transfer faults are often strike-slip and connect normal fault segments.

items.title: estilo de deformação
items.text: estilo de deformação. deformation style. The characterization of the style of deformation of a rock body. Top concept of the deformation style vocabulary.

items.title: frágil (rúptil)
items.text: frágil (rúptil). brittle. Deformation characterized by fracturing and faulting with minimal or no plastic flow. Rock fails by loss of cohesion rather than by continuous deformation. Dominant at shallow crustal levels and low temperatures.

items.title: cataclástico
items.text: cataclástico. cataclastic. Brittle deformation producing a fault rock in which the original grain structure is disrupted by fracturing, crushing and comminution of grains. Includes fault breccia and cataclasite.

items.title: fraturado
items.text: fraturado. fractured. Rock pervasively cut by fractures (joints, veins, or faults) without significant displacement. Fracturing enhances secondary porosity and permeability in reservoir rocks.

items.title: brecha de falha
items.text: brecha de falha. fault breccia. A fault rock containing angular fragments (clasts) greater than 2 mm set in a finer-grained matrix, formed by brittle crushing along a fault zone. Indicates cataclastic deformation at shallow crustal levels.

items.title: milonito
items.text: milonito. mylonite. A ductile fault rock with a well-developed foliation and fine-grained matrix formed by crystal-plastic deformation mechanisms. Indicates high-temperature, deep-crustal shear zones.

items.title: dúctil
items.text: dúctil. ductile. Deformation characterized by flow and plastic deformation without fracturing, producing continuous structures such as folds and foliations. Dominant at high temperatures and pressures (deep crustal levels).

items.title: foliação (estilo)
items.text: foliação (estilo). foliation. Ductile deformation producing a pervasive planar fabric in the rock, including schistosity, cleavage, banding, or gneissic layering. Indicates sustained ductile shear or metamorphic recrystallization.

items.title: boudinage
items.text: boudinage. boudinage. Ductile deformation producing sausage-shaped segments (boudins) of a competent layer within a less competent matrix, resulting from layer-parallel extension. Indicates ductile stretching.

items.title: transição rúptil-dúctil
items.text: transição rúptil-dúctil. brittle-ductile transition. Deformation occurring at conditions intermediate between purely brittle and purely ductile, where both fracturing and crystal-plastic deformation mechanisms operate. Corresponds to seismogenic zone base in continental crust (~15–25 km depth).

items.title: dobrado
items.text: dobrado. folded. Deformation producing folds — curviplanar structures in originally planar features (bedding, foliation). Includes anticlines, synclines, chevron folds, and recumbent folds. Formed by layer-parallel shortening.

items.title: não deformado
items.text: não deformado. undeformed. Rock body that has not undergone significant deformation after its original formation. Retains primary depositional or crystallization structures without overprinting by tectonic deformation.

items.title: contato
items.text: contato. contact. A surface or narrow zone separating two geological units of different lithology, age, or origin. The fundamental unit of geological mapping. Top concept of the contact type vocabulary.

items.title: contato litológico
items.text: contato litológico. lithological contact. A contact between geological units of different lithological character. The boundary may be gradational or sharp and does not necessarily imply a time gap or structural event.

items.title: contato estratigráfico
items.text: contato estratigráfico. stratigraphic contact. A contact between strata that have a defined stratigraphic relationship — either conformable (continuous deposition) or unconformable (depositional break or erosion surface).

items.title: contato concordante
items.text: contato concordante. conformable contact. A stratigraphic contact where the beds above and below are parallel and there is no significant break in sedimentation. Indicates continuous or near-continuous deposition.

items.title: contato discordante
items.text: contato discordante. unconformable contact. A stratigraphic contact representing a significant time gap (hiatus) in the rock record, caused by erosion or non-deposition. The unconformity surface records a period of geological history not represented by strata.

items.title: discordância angular
items.text: discordância angular. angular unconformity. An unconformity in which younger strata overlie an erosion surface on tilted or folded older rocks. The beds above and below are not parallel. Represents a cycle of deposition, deformation, erosion, and renewed deposition.

items.title: disconformidade
items.text: disconformidade. disconformity. An unconformity in which the bedding planes above and below are parallel but an erosion surface separates them, representing a period of erosion or non-deposition without tilting.

items.title: não-conformidade
items.text: não-conformidade. nonconformity. An unconformity where stratified sedimentary rocks overlie igneous or metamorphic rocks. The contact represents a period of uplift, erosion of the older crystalline basement, and subsequent burial by sediments.

items.title: paraconformidade
items.text: paraconformidade. paraconformity. An unconformity in which the strata above and below are parallel and the contact appears conformable but represents a significant time gap identified by paleontological or geochemical evidence.

items.title: contato intrusivo
items.text: contato intrusivo. intrusive contact. A contact between an intrusive igneous rock body (e.g., dyke, sill, pluton) and the country rock it has intruded. May show chilled margins, baked aureoles, and cross-cutting relationships.

items.title: contato metamórfico
items.text: contato metamórfico. metamorphic contact. A contact bounding a metamorphic unit, separating rocks of different metamorphic grade or between metamorphic and non-metamorphic rocks. May be gradational (isogrades) or sharp (thrust-related).

items.title: contato de falha
items.text: contato de falha. faulted contact. A contact produced by faulting, where rock units on either side have been juxtaposed by tectonic displacement rather than by original depositional or intrusive relationships.

items.title: unidade estratigráfica
items.text: unidade estratigráfica. stratigraphic unit. A body of rock established as a distinct entity in the classification of the Earth's rocks based on any of the various stratigraphic procedures (lithostratigraphic, chronostratigraphic, biostratigraphic, etc.). Top concept.

items.title: éon
items.text: éon. eon. The largest division of geologic time, defined by the International Commission on Stratigraphy. The Phanerozoic and Proterozoic are eons. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is eonothem.

items.title: superéon
items.text: superéon. supereon. A subdivision of geologic time larger than an eon. The Precambrian is informally used as a supereon. Rarely used in formal stratigraphic classification.

items.title: era
items.text: era. era. A division of geologic time that is a subdivision of an eon. Examples include the Paleozoic, Mesozoic, and Cenozoic eras. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is erathem.

items.title: período
items.text: período. period. A subdivision of geologic time that is a subdivision of an era. Examples include the Cretaceous, Jurassic, and Triassic periods. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is system.

items.title: época
items.text: época. epoch. A subdivision of geologic time that is a subdivision of a period. Examples include the Early Cretaceous, Late Jurassic. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is series.

items.title: idade
items.text: idade. age. The smallest subdivision of geologic time in formal chronostratigraphic classification, subdividing an epoch. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is stage. Examples: Aptian, Albian, Cenomanian.

items.title: subidade
items.text: subidade. subage. A subdivision of an age in geochronologic classification. Rarely used formally. Corresponds to substage in chronostratigraphy.

items.title: zona
items.text: zona. zone. A body of strata characterized by a particular fossil taxon or assemblage, or by a geochemical signature. Used in biostratigraphic, chemostratigraphic, and magnetostratigraphic classification.

items.title: cronozona
items.text: cronozona. chronozone. The body of rocks formed during the time represented by a polarity chron or biozone; the smallest formal unit of chronostratigraphic classification above the bed level.

items.title: andar
items.text: andar. stage. The fundamental unit of chronostratigraphic classification, representing rock formed during an age. Examples: Aptian Stage, Albian Stage. Defined by boundary stratotype sections (GSSPs).

items.title: subandar
items.text: subandar. substage. A subdivision of a stage in chronostratigraphic classification. Corresponds to subage in geochronologic terminology.

items.title: supersistema
items.text: supersistema. supersystem. A chronostratigraphic unit larger than a system, corresponding to an erathem. Rarely used in current practice.

items.title: sistema
items.text: sistema. system. A chronostratigraphic unit corresponding to a period in geochronology. The fundamental unit of the geological time scale. Examples: Cretaceous System, Jurassic System.

items.title: subsistema
items.text: subsistema. subsystem. A subdivision of a system in chronostratigraphic classification. Corresponds to a subdivision of a period. Used in some regional stratigraphic schemes.

items.title: superseries
items.text: superseries. superseries. A chronostratigraphic unit larger than a series, grouping two or more series. Corresponds to a subdivision of an erathem.

items.title: séries
items.text: séries. series. A chronostratigraphic unit corresponding to an epoch in geochronology. Represents rock deposited during an epoch. Examples: Lower Cretaceous Series, Upper Jurassic Series.

items.title: subséries
items.text: subséries. subseries. A subdivision of a series in chronostratigraphic classification. Corresponds to a subdivision of an epoch.

items.title: supergrupo
items.text: supergrupo. supergroup. A lithostratigraphic unit comprising two or more groups with significant lithological or genetic unity. Used for major basin-filling sequences. Examples: Gondwana Supergroup.

items.title: grupo
items.text: grupo. group. A lithostratigraphic unit comprising two or more associated formations with significant lithological unity. Defines a major depositional cycle or phase within a basin.

items.title: subgrupo
items.text: subgrupo. subgroup. A lithostratigraphic unit that is a subdivision of a group, comprising two or more formations. Used when a group needs further subdivision.

items.title: formação
items.text: formação. formation. The fundamental unit of lithostratigraphic classification. A body of rock strata with consistent lithological characteristics that make it possible to recognize and map it at the surface or in the subsurface. Examples: Barra Velha Formation, Carapebus Formation.

items.title: membro
items.text: membro. member. A lithostratigraphic unit that is a named subdivision of a formation, distinguished by a particular lithological character. Not all formations are divided into members; a member occurs only within a single formation.

items.title: camada
items.text: camada. bed. The smallest lithostratigraphic unit, representing a single layer of rock with observable internal physical homogeneity. Beds are the fundamental units of sedimentary sequences.

items.title: unidade litoestratigráfica
items.text: unidade litoestratigráfica. lithostratigraphic unit. A body of rock that is defined and characterized on the basis of its lithological properties and stratigraphic position. The hierarchy includes supergroup, group, subgroup, formation, member, and bed.

items.title: unidade bioestratigráfica
items.text: unidade bioestratigráfica. biostratigraphic unit. A body of rock defined by its fossil content (biozone), characterized by the occurrence of one or more particular taxa. Biozones are the fundamental biostratigraphic units used for correlation.

items.title: unidade quimioestratigráfica
items.text: unidade quimioestratigráfica. chemostratigraphic unit. A body of rock characterized by a distinctive geochemical signature (isotopic ratios, trace element concentrations) used for correlation and paleoenvironmental reconstruction. Examples: carbon isotope excursion units, strontium isotope zones.

items.title: unidade magnetoestratigráfica
items.text: unidade magnetoestratigráfica. magnetostratigraphic unit. A body of rock defined by its remanent magnetization polarity. Magnetic polarity zones (magnetozones) are used for correlation with the global geomagnetic polarity timescale (GPTS).
